skill.name: fiftyone-find-duplicates
skill.description: Find duplicate and near-duplicate images in FiftyOne datasets with Brain similarity operators, review results in the FiftyOne App, and deduplicate selected samples.
skill.summary: Find, review, and remove duplicate images in FiftyOne datasets.
skill.author: AdonaiVera

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows these are Markdown code fences, inline operator names, table separators, and ordinary documentation text. No malicious prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration was found. The remaining risks are legitimate but user-sensitive workflows: installing a FiftyOne plugin or dependency and deleting duplicate samples.

medium_findings.title: User-mediated plugin and dependency installation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to download and enable the @voxel51/brain plugin and to offer running an install command returned for missing dependencies. This appears legitimate for FiftyOne functionality, but any plugin or package installation can introduce supply-chain risk and should require explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installation guidance is explicit and semantically relevant. The referenced plugin is a known FiftyOne plugin source, so the concern is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Destructive dataset deduplication workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill documents operators that delete exact or near-duplicate samples after review. This is expected behavior for a deduplication skill, but it can remove dataset records if used without careful review or backups.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited operators are explicitly named as deduplication or deletion actions. The surrounding text recommends review, which lowers severity but does not remove the data-loss risk.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution detections are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer classified Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The cited locations are code fences, inline tool names, thresholds, and examples for FiftyOne MCP operator calls, not executable Ruby code embedded in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows Markdown formatting and Python or JSON examples only. No executable Ruby source or shell backtick invocation exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Static weak-cryptography detections are text false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits align with natural-language duplicate-detection text and Markdown table syntax, not use of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or another cryptographic primitive. No evidence found of cryptographic operations in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain description text, table headers, or field names such as install_command. They do not contain cryptographic API usage or weak algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL detections are documentation links
low_findings.description: The static URL hits are a localhost FiftyOne App URL and public documentation or source links. They do not receive secrets, transmit dataset contents, or implement network callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references or local app guidance. No code in the skill sends data to these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package installation suggested from tool output
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting section tells the assistant to offer running an install command reported by the MCP server. This should remain an explicit user-approved action because package installation changes the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill only says to offer the install command, not execute it silently. Risk depends on the trustworthiness of the MCP server response and user approval flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dataset deletion operators documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented deduplication operators can remove samples from a FiftyOne dataset. This is core functionality but should be preceded by review, backups, or a dry run where available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The operator names and surrounding instructions clearly describe deduplication and deletion. The documented review step reduces but does not eliminate risk.

content.value_statement: Duplicate images can waste storage and bias model training. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through FiftyOne Brain workflows to find, review, and remove redundant samples.
actual_capabilities: Sets the active FiftyOne dataset context before running duplicate workflows.
Launches the FiftyOne App for delegated Brain operators and review steps.
Discovers available Brain operators and retrieves their current schemas.
Computes image similarity indexes with configurable brain keys and models.
Finds exact or near-duplicate image groups using FiftyOne Brain operators.
Guides review, filtering, saved views, and deduplication cleanup steps.
limitations: Requires a running FiftyOne MCP server and an image dataset loaded in FiftyOne.
Requires the @voxel51/brain plugin and any model dependencies needed by FiftyOne.
Similarity quality depends on the selected model, threshold, and dataset content.
Deletion workflows can remove samples, so users should review results before deduplication.

use_cases.title: Clean training datasets
use_cases.description: Remove duplicate images before model training to reduce bias and wasted compute.

use_cases.title: Review visual redundancy
use_cases.description: Find near-duplicate groups and inspect them in the FiftyOne App before deletion.

use_cases.title: Audit dataset quality
use_cases.description: Measure similarity patterns and locate repeated media during computer vision dataset reviews.

prompt_templates.title: Find duplicates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the FiftyOne duplicate-finding workflow on my active image dataset. Show me the duplicate groups before deleting anything.

prompt_templates.title: Review near duplicates
prompt_templates.prompt: Compute image similarity with a reasonable default model, find near duplicates at threshold 0.3, and open the saved view for review.

prompt_templates.title: Remove exact duplicates
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my FiftyOne dataset for exact duplicate media files. Explain the expected changes and ask before running any deduplication operator.

prompt_templates.title: Compare similarity settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a cautious duplicate analysis with separate brain keys for strict and broad thresholds. Summarize the tradeoffs before any deletion step.

output_examples.input: Find near duplicates in my dataset and show them for review.
output_examples.output: The workflow sets the dataset context, launches the FiftyOne App, computes a similarity index, finds near-duplicate groups, and opens a filtered view for inspection.

output_examples.input: Remove exact duplicate image files after I confirm.
output_examples.output: The workflow checks for exact duplicates, reports that the deduplication step keeps representatives, and waits for confirmation before deletion.

output_examples.input: My similarity operator says the brain key is missing.
output_examples.output: The workflow explains that embeddings must be computed first, then reruns similarity computation with a named brain key before duplicate search.

best_practices: Discover operator schemas before execution because plugin parameters can change.
Review duplicate views in the FiftyOne App before deleting samples.
Use stable brain keys so similarity indexes can be reused across related operations.
anti_patterns: Do not run deduplication before inspecting duplicate groups or saved views.
Do not assume a threshold works for every dataset or embedding model.
Do not install missing dependencies without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill require FiftyOne?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires a running FiftyOne MCP server and a loaded image dataset.

faq.question: Does it delete images automatically?
faq.answer: No. The workflow includes deletion operators, but users should review results and confirm first.

faq.question: Can it find exact duplicates?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents exact duplicate operators for identical media files.

faq.question: Can it find visually similar images?
faq.answer: Yes. It computes similarity embeddings and finds near duplicates using configurable thresholds.

faq.question: What plugin is needed?
faq.answer: The workflow depends on the @voxel51/brain plugin for Brain similarity operators.

faq.question: What should I do before deleting duplicates?
faq.answer: Open the duplicate view, inspect the groups, and keep a backup or recovery path for important datasets.
